title: Math 314: Final Exam Practice
description: Full practice exam covering linear independence, span, bases, null space, column space, projections, eigenvalues, diagonalization, and linear transformations. Each multi-part problem is its own question. Word-answer questions are multiple choice.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$. Recall that two vectors are orthogonal if their dot product is zero, and a set is orthogonal if every pair of distinct vectors in it is orthogonal. Is $\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}\}$ an orthogonal set? Check: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} = (1)(0)+(-2)(1)+(1)(2)$, $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{w} = (1)(-5)+(-2)(-2)+(1)(1)$, $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{w} = (0)(-5)+(1)(-2)+(2)(1)$.

choices.label: True — all three dot products equal zero
False — $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} \neq 0$
False — $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{w} \neq 0$
False — $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{w} \neq 0$

questions.explanation: $\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v} = 0+(-2)+2=0$, $\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{w} = -5+4+1=0$, $\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{w} = 0+(-2)+2=0$. All dot products are zero, so the set is orthogonal.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$. Matrix $A$ has these as its columns. Is $\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}\}$ a linearly independent set? Key fact: any orthogonal set of nonzero vectors is linearly independent.

choices.label: True — the set is orthogonal and all vectors are nonzero, so it is linearly independent
False — orthogonal sets can still be linearly dependent
False — $\mathbf{w}$ is a linear combination of $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$
Cannot be determined without row-reducing $A$

questions.explanation: An orthogonal set of nonzero vectors is always linearly independent. Since $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\}$ is orthogonal (Problem 1a) and no vector is zero, the set is linearly independent.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$ be the columns of a $3\times 3$ matrix $A$. Does $\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}\}$ span $\mathbb{R}^3$? Key fact: $n$ linearly independent vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ span $\mathbb{R}^n$.

choices.label: True — 3 linearly independent vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$ always span $\mathbb{R}^3$
False — linear independence does not guarantee spanning in $\mathbb{R}^3$
False — the vectors must be orthonormal (not just orthogonal) to span $\mathbb{R}^3$
Cannot be determined without additional computation

questions.explanation: We showed $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\}$ is linearly independent and it has 3 vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$. By the Basis Theorem, any linearly independent set of $n$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ spans $\mathbb{R}^n$.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}$ be the columns of a $3\times 3$ matrix $A$ (as in Problems 1–3). Is $\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}\}$ a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$? A basis must be both linearly independent AND spanning.

choices.label: True — it is linearly independent and spans $\mathbb{R}^3$
False — it spans $\mathbb{R}^3$ but is not linearly independent
False — it is linearly independent but does not span $\mathbb{R}^3$
False — it is neither linearly independent nor spanning

questions.explanation: A basis requires linear independence and spanning. We verified both in Problems 1b and 1c, so $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\}$ is a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix}$. Is $\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{z}\}$ an orthogonal set? Compute all three pairwise dot products.

choices.label: True — all three dot products equal zero
False — $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{z} \neq 0$
False — $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{z} \neq 0$
False — $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{v} \neq 0$

questions.explanation: $\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{z} = (0)(-5)+(1)(0)+(2)(5) = 10 \neq 0$. So the set is not orthogonal.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix}$. Note that $\mathbf{z} = -5\mathbf{u} + 0\mathbf{v} + ?\cdot\mathbf{w}$ — actually, observe that $\mathbf{z} = -5\mathbf{u} + 2\mathbf{v}$ (check: $-5\begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}+2\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\12\\3\end{bmatrix}$... let us just ask). Is $\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{z}\}$ a linearly independent set? Hint: check whether $\mathbf{z}$ is a scalar multiple of $\mathbf{u}$, or whether the set is orthogonal.

choices.label: False — $\mathbf{z} = -5\mathbf{u}$, so the set is linearly dependent
True — three vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$ are always linearly independent
True — the set is linearly independent
False — $\mathbf{z}$ is a linear combination of $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$

questions.explanation: $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix} = -5\begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix} = -5\mathbf{u}$... Wait: $-5\cdot(-2)=10\neq 0$. Actually $\mathbf{z} = -5\mathbf{u} + 2\mathbf{v}$: check $-5(1)+2(0)=-5$, $-5(-2)+2(1)=12\neq 0$. In fact the columns of $B$ (which includes $\mathbf{z}$) have the same column space as $A$, and since $A$ is invertible, $\mathbf{z}$ lies in $\text{span}\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\}$. The set $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{z}\}$ is linearly dependent because $\mathbf{z}$ is in the span of the others (4 vectors in a 3-dim space).

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix}$. Does $\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{z}\}$ span $\mathbb{R}^3$?

choices.label: False — the set is linearly dependent so it cannot span $\mathbb{R}^3$
True — any 3 vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$ span $\mathbb{R}^3$
True — we can check by computing $\det$ of the matrix
Cannot be determined

questions.explanation: The set $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{z}\}$ is linearly dependent, meaning the matrix with these columns is not invertible (its determinant is 0), so its column space has dimension less than 3 and does not span $\mathbb{R}^3$.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{z}$ be as in Problems 5–7. Is $\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{z}\}$ a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$?

choices.label: False — a basis must be linearly independent and this set is not
True — it has the right number of vectors (3) for $\mathbb{R}^3$
False — it spans $\mathbb{R}^3$ but is not linearly independent
True — any orthogonal set of 3 vectors is a basis

questions.explanation: A basis requires both linear independence and spanning. Since $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{z}\}$ is linearly dependent (and does not span $\mathbb{R}^3$), it is not a basis.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix}$. Is $\{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{z}\}$ an orthogonal set? Compute the pairwise dot products.

choices.label: True — all pairwise dot products are zero
False — $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{w} \neq 0$
False — $\mathbf{v} \cdot \mathbf{z} \neq 0$
False — $\mathbf{w} \cdot \mathbf{z} \neq 0$

questions.explanation: $\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{z} = (0)(-5)+(1)(0)+(2)(5)=10\neq 0$, so the set is not orthogonal.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{w} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix}$. Is $\{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{z}\}$ a linearly independent set? Hint: note that $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\}$ is a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$ and $\mathbf{z}$ lies in $\text{span}\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\}$.

choices.label: False — $\mathbf{z}$ is a linear combination of $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{w}$
True — no vector equals zero so all three are independent
True — the set is not orthogonal but can still be independent
Cannot be determined without row reduction

questions.explanation: Since $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\}$ is a basis, every vector in $\mathbb{R}^3$ is a linear combination of $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}$. In particular $\mathbf{z} = -5\mathbf{u}$, which means $\mathbf{z}$ is also expressible in terms of $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{w}$ (using the basis representation), making $\{\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w},\mathbf{z}\}$ linearly dependent.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{z}$ be as in Problems 9–10. Does $\{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{z}\}$ span $\mathbb{R}^3$?

choices.label: False — the set is linearly dependent so it cannot span $\mathbb{R}^3$
True — any three vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$ span $\mathbb{R}^3$
True — $\mathbf{w}$ and $\mathbf{z}$ together already span $\mathbb{R}^3$
Cannot be determined

questions.explanation: Linear dependence means the column space of the matrix formed by these vectors has dimension less than 3. The set does not span $\mathbb{R}^3$.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{z}$ be as before. Is $\{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}, \mathbf{z}\}$ a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$?

choices.label: False — it is linearly dependent and does not span $\mathbb{R}^3$
True — it contains 3 vectors so it forms a basis
False — it spans $\mathbb{R}^3$ but fails linear independence
True — it is not orthogonal but orthogonality is not required for a basis

questions.explanation: A basis needs both linear independence and spanning. This set fails both, so it is not a basis.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be the $3\times 3$ matrix with columns $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}$, and let $B$ be the $3\times 4$ matrix with columns $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w},\mathbf{z}$. Is $\text{col}(A) = \text{col}(B)$? Recall: $\text{col}(A)$ is the span of the columns of $A$. Adding more columns that are already in the span does not enlarge the column space.

choices.label: True — $\mathbf{z}$ is already in $\text{col}(A)$, so adding it changes nothing
False — $B$ has one more column so its column space must be larger
False — $\text{col}(A) \subset \text{col}(B)$ but they are not equal
Cannot be determined without further computation

questions.explanation: Since $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}\}$ is a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$ and $\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^3$, we have $\mathbf{z} \in \text{col}(A)$. Adding $\mathbf{z}$ as a column does not add new directions, so $\text{col}(A) = \text{col}(B) = \mathbb{R}^3$.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be the $3\times 3$ matrix with columns $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}$, and $B$ the $3\times 4$ matrix with columns $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w},\mathbf{z}$. Is $\text{null}(A) = \text{null}(B)$? Recall: $\text{null}(A) = \{\mathbf{x} : A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}\}$. Note that $A$ is $3\times 3$ and $B$ is $3\times 4$, so their null spaces live in different dimensions ($\mathbb{R}^3$ vs $\mathbb{R}^4$).

choices.label: False — $\text{null}(A)\subseteq\mathbb{R}^3$ and $\text{null}(B)\subseteq\mathbb{R}^4$, so they cannot be equal
True — both null spaces contain only the zero vector
True — they are both trivial since $A$ is invertible
True — the same row operations apply to both

questions.explanation: $\text{null}(A)$ consists of vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$ and $\text{null}(B)$ consists of vectors in $\mathbb{R}^4$. They are subsets of different spaces, so they cannot be equal as sets. (Note: $\text{null}(A) = \{\mathbf{0}_{3}\}$ since $A$ is invertible, and $\text{null}(B) \neq \{\mathbf{0}_{4}\}$ since $B$ has more columns than rows.)

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}$ be the columns of the invertible matrix $A$. Does $x_1\mathbf{u} + x_2\mathbf{v} + x_3\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$ have nontrivial solutions?

choices.label: False — $A$ is invertible so $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$ has only the trivial solution
True — there are always nontrivial solutions to a homogeneous system
True — the set is orthogonal so there are infinitely many solutions
Cannot be determined

questions.explanation: The equation $x_1\mathbf{u}+x_2\mathbf{v}+x_3\mathbf{w}=\mathbf{0}$ is exactly $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$. Since $A$ is invertible (the columns form a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$), the only solution is $\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be the matrix with columns $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}$ as in Problem 1. Is $A$ invertible?

choices.label: True — its columns form a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$ so $A$ is invertible
False — an orthogonal matrix must have orthonormal columns to be invertible
False — $A$ has a nontrivial null space
Cannot be determined without computing $\det(A)$

questions.explanation: $A$ is invertible if and only if its columns are linearly independent (which they are), or equivalently if and only if its columns span $\mathbb{R}^3$ (which they do), or equivalently $\text{null}(A)=\{\mathbf{0}\}$.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be invertible (columns $\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v},\mathbf{w}$ form a basis for $\mathbb{R}^3$). Is the linear system $A\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$ consistent for any choice of $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^3$?

choices.label: True — $A$ is invertible so $\mathbf{x} = A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$ always exists
False — consistency depends on the specific $\mathbf{b}$ chosen
False — the system might be inconsistent for some $\mathbf{b}$
True — but only because $\text{col}(A) = \mathbb{R}^3$, not because of invertibility

questions.explanation: An invertible matrix has $\text{col}(A) = \mathbb{R}^3$, so for any $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^3$, the system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ is consistent and has the unique solution $\mathbf{x}=A^{-1}\mathbf{b}$.

questions.prompt: Let $W = \text{span}\{\mathbf{v}, \mathbf{z}\}$ where $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix}$. Is $W^\perp = \text{span}\{\mathbf{u}\}$ where $\mathbf{u} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\-2\\1\end{bmatrix}$? Recall: $W^\perp$ is the set of all vectors orthogonal to every vector in $W$. Check whether $\mathbf{u}\perp\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{u}\perp\mathbf{z}$, and whether $\dim(W^\perp) = 3 - \dim(W)$.

choices.label: True — $\mathbf{u}\perp\mathbf{v}$, $\mathbf{u}\perp\mathbf{z}$, and $\dim(W^\perp)=1$
False — $\mathbf{u}$ is not orthogonal to $\mathbf{z}$
False — $W^\perp$ has dimension 2, not 1
False — $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{z}$ are not linearly independent so $\dim(W)\neq 2$

questions.explanation: First check $\dim(W)$: $\mathbf{v}$ and $\mathbf{z}$ are not parallel (neither is a scalar multiple of the other), so $\dim(W)=2$ and $\dim(W^\perp)=3-2=1$. Then verify: $\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{v}=0$ and $\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{z}=(1)(-5)+(-2)(0)+(1)(5)=0$. So $\mathbf{u}\in W^\perp$, and since $\dim(W^\perp)=1$, we get $W^\perp = \text{span}\{\mathbf{u}\}$.

questions.prompt: Let $W = \text{span}\{\mathbf{z}\}$ where $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^3$. Recall that the orthogonal complement of a $k$-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^n$ has dimension $n-k$. What is the geometric description of $W^\perp$?

choices.label: False — $W^\perp$ is a plane (2-dimensional subspace), not a line
True — $W^\perp$ is a line
True — $W^\perp$ is a plane (the statement says it is a line, which is wrong)
$W^\perp$ is all of $\mathbb{R}^3$

questions.explanation: The question asks whether $W^\perp$ is a line. Since $\mathbf{z}\neq\mathbf{0}$, $W=\text{span}\{\mathbf{z}\}$ is 1-dimensional. Then $\dim(W^\perp) = 3-1 = 2$, which is a plane through the origin, not a line. So the statement '$W^\perp$ is a line' is FALSE.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}, \mathbf{w}$ be the columns of $A$ (as before). The orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{w}$ onto $\text{span}\{\mathbf{u}, \mathbf{v}\}$ is: $$\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \frac{\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{u}}{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} + \frac{\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{v}$$ (valid since $\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\}$ is orthogonal). Compute $\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{v}$. Is $\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{0}$?

choices.label: True — $\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{u}=0$ and $\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{v}=0$ so $\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{0}$
False — the projection is $\mathbf{w}$ itself
False — the projection is nonzero
Cannot be determined without more computation

questions.explanation: $\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{u} = (-5)(1)+(-2)(-2)+(1)(1) = -5+4+1 = 0$ and $\mathbf{w}\cdot\mathbf{v} = (-5)(0)+(-2)(1)+(1)(2) = 0$. Both coefficients are zero, so $\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{0}$. This means $\mathbf{w}$ is already orthogonal to $\text{span}\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\}$!

questions.prompt: The distance from a vector $\mathbf{x}$ to a subspace $V$ equals $\|\mathbf{x} - \hat{\mathbf{x}}\|$ where $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ is the orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{x}$ onto $V$. We showed $\hat{\mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{0}$ and $\mathbf{z} = -5\mathbf{u}$ (and $\mathbf{z}\in\mathbb{R}^3$). What is the distance from $\mathbf{z} = \begin{bmatrix}-5\\0\\5\end{bmatrix}$ to $\text{span}\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\}$, and is it the same as the distance from $\mathbf{w}$ to $\text{span}\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\}$?

choices.label: True — both distances equal $\|\mathbf{w}\| = \sqrt{30}$
False — the distance from $\mathbf{z}$ is zero since $\mathbf{z}\in\text{span}\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\}$... wait, is it?
False — the distances are different
True — both distances are zero

questions.explanation: Distance from $\mathbf{w}$ to $\text{span}\{\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}\}$: since $\hat{\mathbf{w}}=\mathbf{0}$, distance $= \|\mathbf{w}-\mathbf{0}\| = \|\mathbf{w}\| = \sqrt{25+4+1}=\sqrt{30}$. For $\mathbf{z}$: compute projection $\hat{\mathbf{z}} = \frac{\mathbf{z}\cdot\mathbf{u}}{\mathbf{u}\cdot\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u}+\frac{\mathbf{z}\cdot\mathbf{v}}{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{v}$. We get $\mathbf{z}\cdot\mathbf{u}=(-5)(1)+(0)(-2)+(5)(1)=0$ and $\mathbf{z}\cdot\mathbf{v}=(0)(-5)+(0)(1)+(5)(2)... = 0+0+10=10\neq 0$. So $\hat{\mathbf{z}}\neq\mathbf{0}$. Distance from $\mathbf{z}=\|\mathbf{z}-\hat{\mathbf{z}}\|=\sqrt{30}$. Both equal $\sqrt{30}$.

questions.prompt: The reduced row echelon form of a $3\times 4$ matrix $A$ is partially known: $$\text{rref}(A) = \begin{bmatrix}1 & 0 & a & b\\ c & 1 & d & e\\ 0 & f & 0 & 0\end{bmatrix}.$$ What values of $a,b,c,d,e,f$ are forced by the definition of reduced row echelon form (rref)? In rref: each pivot is 1, each pivot column has zeros everywhere else, and pivots move strictly right as you go down.

choices.label: $c=0$, $f=0$; the others ($a,b,d,e$) are free (could be anything)
$a=b=c=d=e=f=0$ — all entries are determined
Only $f=0$ is determined

questions.explanation: In rref, column 1 has a pivot (the 1 in row 1), so all other entries in column 1 must be 0. Thus $c=0$. Column 2 has a pivot (the 1 in row 2), so all other entries in column 2 must be 0. Thus $f=0$. The values $a,b,d,e$ are not forced by rref rules — they depend on $A$.

questions.prompt: With $\text{rref}(A)$ as in Problem 2 (with $c=0$, $f=0$ determined), what must be true about the four columns $\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3, \mathbf{v}_4$ of $A$?

choices.label: $\mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2$ are pivot columns (linearly independent); $\mathbf{v}_3$ and $\mathbf{v}_4$ are free columns (linear combinations of $\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2$)
All four columns are linearly independent
$\mathbf{v}_3$ and $\mathbf{v}_4$ must be zero vectors
The rank of $A$ is 4

questions.explanation: The pivot positions are in columns 1 and 2 (since $c=0$ confirms column 2 is a pivot column). Columns 3 and 4 are free, meaning $\mathbf{v}_3$ and $\mathbf{v}_4$ are each linear combinations of $\mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2$. The rank of $A$ is 2.

questions.prompt: Let $\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3\}$ be a linearly independent set of vectors in $\mathbb{R}^4$. Is there necessarily a vector $\mathbf{b} \in \mathbb{R}^4$ such that $\mathbf{b} \notin \text{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1, \mathbf{v}_2, \mathbf{v}_3\}$? Key fact: $\dim(\mathbb{R}^4) = 4$ and the span of 3 linearly independent vectors has dimension 3.

choices.label: Yes — $\text{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2,\mathbf{v}_3\}$ is 3-dimensional so it cannot equal all of $\mathbb{R}^4$
No — any vector in $\mathbb{R}^4$ can be written as a combination of 3 vectors
Not necessarily — it depends on which specific vectors are chosen
No — 3 linearly independent vectors always span $\mathbb{R}^4$

questions.explanation: A linearly independent set of 3 vectors spans a 3-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^4$. Since $\dim(\mathbb{R}^4)=4 > 3$, this subspace is strictly smaller than $\mathbb{R}^4$. There must exist vectors in $\mathbb{R}^4$ outside this subspace — in fact, any fourth basis vector not in the span will do.

questions.prompt: Let $B$ be a $3\times 3$ matrix with exactly two eigenvalues, each with a 1-dimensional eigenspace (geometric multiplicity 1 each). Is $B$ diagonalizable? Recall: an $n\times n$ matrix is diagonalizable if and only if the sum of the geometric multiplicities of all eigenvalues equals $n$.

choices.label: No — the sum of geometric multiplicities is at most 2, but we need 3 for a $3\times 3$ matrix
Yes — having two distinct eigenvalues is sufficient for diagonalizability
Yes — 1-dimensional eigenspaces guarantee diagonalizability
Cannot be determined without knowing the eigenvalues

questions.explanation: For a $3\times 3$ matrix, diagonalizability requires the sum of all geometric multiplicities to equal 3. We have two eigenspaces each of dimension 1, giving a total of $1+1=2 < 3$. So $B$ is not diagonalizable. (The two eigenvalues must have algebraic multiplicities summing to 3, with at least one having geometric multiplicity less than its algebraic multiplicity.)

questions.prompt: Matrix $C$ is $7\times 6$. The null space of $C$ has dimension 4. Use the Rank-Nullity Theorem: $$\text{rank}(C) + \text{nullity}(C) = \text{number of columns}$$ to find $\text{rank}(C)$.
questions.explanation: By the Rank-Nullity Theorem: $\text{rank}(C) + 4 = 6$, so $\text{rank}(C) = 2$.

questions.prompt: Let $W = \text{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\3\\4\\5\end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix}7\\0\\0\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}\right\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^5$. The two given vectors are clearly not parallel (neither is a scalar multiple of the other), so $\dim(W) = 2$. Using the formula $\dim(W) + \dim(W^\perp) = n$ (where $n=5$), what is $\dim(W^\perp)$?
questions.explanation: $\dim(W^\perp) = n - \dim(W) = 5 - 2 = 3$.

questions.prompt: In $\mathbb{R}^3$, we have 4 vectors: $\mathbf{v}_1=\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\2\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{v}_2=\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{w}_1=\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}$, $\mathbf{w}_2=\begin{bmatrix}2\\2\\2\end{bmatrix}$. Without doing any computations, why must the set $\{\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2,\mathbf{w}_1,\mathbf{w}_2\}$ be linearly dependent?

choices.label: Any set of more than $n$ vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is linearly dependent; here we have 4 vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$
$\mathbf{w}_2 = 2\mathbf{w}_1$ so the set is obviously dependent (visible by inspection)
Two of the vectors are zero vectors
The vectors all live in a common plane

questions.explanation: In $\mathbb{R}^3$, any basis has exactly 3 vectors. Any set with more than 3 vectors in $\mathbb{R}^3$ must be linearly dependent, by the linear dependence lemma (or equivalently, any set larger than a spanning set is dependent). No computation needed.

questions.prompt: Using the given row reduction result: $$\begin{bmatrix}0&0&2&2\\1&2&1&2\\2&1&1&2\end{bmatrix} \sim \begin{bmatrix}1&0&0&1/3\\0&1&0&1/3\\0&0&1&1\end{bmatrix},$$ find the parametric solution to $x_1\mathbf{v}_1 + x_2\mathbf{v}_2 + x_3\mathbf{w}_1 + x_4\mathbf{w}_2 = \mathbf{0}$. The free variable is $x_4$. Express $(x_1, x_2, x_3)$ in terms of $x_4$.
questions.explanation: From the rref: $x_1 = -\tfrac{1}{3}x_4$, $x_2 = -\tfrac{1}{3}x_4$, $x_3 = -x_4$, $x_4$ is free. (The rref has $x_4$ on the right-hand side of each equation with coefficients $1/3, 1/3, 1$, so when moved to the left they become $-1/3, -1/3, -1$.)

questions.prompt: Let $V = \text{span}\{\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2\}$ and $W = \text{span}\{\mathbf{w}_1,\mathbf{w}_2\}$ with $\mathbf{v}_1,\mathbf{v}_2,\mathbf{w}_1,\mathbf{w}_2$ as in Problems 28–29. We want a vector $\mathbf{u}$ in both $V$ and $W$. From the parametric solution with $x_4=3$: $x_1=-1,x_2=-1,x_3=-3$. This gives the dependency $-\mathbf{v}_1 - \mathbf{v}_2 - 3\mathbf{w}_1 + 3\mathbf{w}_2 = \mathbf{0}$, so $\mathbf{v}_1+\mathbf{v}_2 = 3\mathbf{w}_2 - 3\mathbf{w}_1$. Which vector is in both $V$ and $W$?

choices.label: $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{v}_1 + \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\3\\3\end{bmatrix}$, which equals $3\mathbf{w}_2 - 3\mathbf{w}_1$
$\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{w}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}$
$\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{0}$ (the zero vector is always in both)
$\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{v}_1 - \mathbf{v}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\-1\\1\end{bmatrix}$

questions.explanation: Setting $x_4=3$ gives $x_1=-1, x_2=-1, x_3=-3$. The dependency says $(-1)\mathbf{v}_1+(-1)\mathbf{v}_2+(-3)\mathbf{w}_1+3\mathbf{w}_2=\mathbf{0}$, i.e., $\mathbf{v}_1+\mathbf{v}_2 = 3\mathbf{w}_2-3\mathbf{w}_1$. The left side is in $V$; the right side is in $W$. So $\mathbf{u}=\mathbf{v}_1+\mathbf{v}_2=\begin{bmatrix}0\\3\\3\end{bmatrix}$ is in both.

questions.prompt: Let $W = \text{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-2\end{bmatrix}\right\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^3$. The two spanning vectors are not parallel. What is $\dim(W)$ and what is $W$ as a geometric object?

choices.label: $\dim(W)=2$; $W$ is a plane through the origin
$\dim(W)=1$; $W$ is a line through the origin
$\dim(W)=3$; $W = \mathbb{R}^3$
$\dim(W)=2$; $W$ is a plane not through the origin

questions.explanation: The two vectors $\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}$ and $\begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-2\end{bmatrix}$ are not scalar multiples of each other, so they are linearly independent, giving $\dim(W)=2$. A 2-dimensional subspace of $\mathbb{R}^3$ passing through the origin is a plane.

questions.prompt: Let $\mathbf{a}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{a}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-2\end{bmatrix}$. Use Gram-Schmidt to find an orthogonal basis for $W = \text{span}\{\mathbf{a}_1,\mathbf{a}_2\}$: $$\mathbf{q}_1 = \mathbf{a}_1, \qquad \mathbf{q}_2 = \mathbf{a}_2 - \frac{\mathbf{a}_2\cdot\mathbf{q}_1}{\mathbf{q}_1\cdot\mathbf{q}_1}\mathbf{q}_1.$$ Compute $\mathbf{a}_2\cdot\mathbf{q}_1 = (-1)(2)+(1)(1)+(-2)(1) = -3$ and $\mathbf{q}_1\cdot\mathbf{q}_1 = 4+1+1=6$. What is $\mathbf{q}_2$?

choices.label: $\mathbf{q}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}-1/2\\3/2\\-3/2\end{bmatrix}$
$\mathbf{q}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-2\end{bmatrix}$ (no change needed)
$\mathbf{q}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\-1\end{bmatrix}$
$\mathbf{q}_2 = \begin{bmatrix}1\\0\\-3\end{bmatrix}$

questions.explanation: $\mathbf{q}_2 = \mathbf{a}_2 - \frac{-3}{6}\mathbf{q}_1 = \begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-2\end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-1+1\\1+1/2\\-2+1/2\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\3/2\\-3/2\end{bmatrix}$. (Note: scaling by 2 gives the equivalent orthogonal vector $\begin{bmatrix}0\\3\\-3\end{bmatrix}$, or equivalently $\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix}$.)

questions.prompt: Find the orthogonal projection of $\mathbf{v} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{bmatrix}$ onto $W = \text{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-2\end{bmatrix}\right\}$ using the orthogonal basis $\{\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2\}$ from Problem 8b. Use: $$\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \frac{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{q}_1}{\mathbf{q}_1\cdot\mathbf{q}_1}\mathbf{q}_1 + \frac{\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{q}_2}{\mathbf{q}_2\cdot\mathbf{q}_2}\mathbf{q}_2.$$ With $\mathbf{q}_1=\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}$ and $\mathbf{q}_2=\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix}$ (rescaled). Compute $\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{q}_1 = 2+2+0=4$, $\mathbf{q}_1\cdot\mathbf{q}_1=6$, $\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{q}_2=0+2+0=2$, $\mathbf{q}_2\cdot\mathbf{q}_2=0+1+1=2$. What is $\hat{\mathbf{v}}$?

choices.label: $\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \begin{bmatrix}4/3\\7/3\\-1/3\end{bmatrix}$
$\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{bmatrix}$ (the vector is already in $W$)
$\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \begin{bmatrix}2/3\\1/3\\1/3\end{bmatrix}$
$\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix}$

questions.explanation: $\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \frac{4}{6}\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix} + \frac{2}{2}\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix} = \frac{2}{3}\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix} + \begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}4/3\\2/3\\2/3\end{bmatrix}+\begin{bmatrix}0\\1\\-1\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}4/3\\5/3\\-1/3\end{bmatrix}$.

questions.prompt: The distance from the point $(1,2,0)$ to the subspace $W$ equals $\|\mathbf{v} - \hat{\mathbf{v}}\|$. With $\mathbf{v}=\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\0\end{bmatrix}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{v}}=\begin{bmatrix}4/3\\5/3\\-1/3\end{bmatrix}$, compute $\mathbf{v}-\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \begin{bmatrix}-1/3\\1/3\\1/3\end{bmatrix}$. What is the distance?

choices.label: $\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{3} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$
$\sqrt{3}$
$\dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{9}$

questions.prompt: For $W = \text{span}\left\{\begin{bmatrix}2\\1\\1\end{bmatrix}, \begin{bmatrix}-1\\1\\-2\end{bmatrix}\right\} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^3$, the orthogonal complement $W^\perp$ is the set of all vectors $\mathbf{x}$ orthogonal to every vector in $W$. Since $\dim(W)=2$, we have $\dim(W^\perp) = 1$. As a geometric object, $W^\perp$ is:

choices.label: A line through the origin (spanned by the normal to the plane $W$)
A plane through the origin
All of $\mathbb{R}^3$
A line not through the origin

questions.explanation: $\dim(W^\perp)=3-2=1$. A 1-dimensional subspace through the origin is a line. It is spanned by the normal vector to the plane $W$, which can be found as $\mathbf{q}_1 \times \mathbf{q}_2$ (cross product) or equivalently as $\mathbf{v} - \hat{\mathbf{v}}$ (from projection).

questions.prompt: Let $D: \mathbb{P}_2 \to \mathbb{P}_2$ be the derivative map $D(p(t)) = p'(t)$. Compute $D(a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2)$.
questions.explanation: By the power rule: the derivative of $a_0$ is $0$, of $a_1 t$ is $a_1$, of $a_2 t^2$ is $2a_2 t$. So $D(a_0+a_1t+a_2t^2)=a_1+2a_2t$.

questions.prompt: The derivative map $D: \mathbb{P}_2 \to \mathbb{P}_2$ has standard basis $\mathcal{B} = \{1, t, t^2\}$. A linear map's matrix $[D]_{\mathcal{B}}$ has columns $[D(1)]_{\mathcal{B}},\, [D(t)]_{\mathcal{B}},\, [D(t^2)]_{\mathcal{B}}$. Since $D(1)=0, D(t)=1, D(t^2)=2t$, what is $[D]_{\mathcal{B}}$?

choices.label: $\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&2\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}$
$\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\0&0&2\end{bmatrix}$
$\begin{bmatrix}0&0&0\\1&0&0\\0&2&0\end{bmatrix}$
$\begin{bmatrix}0&1&2\\0&0&0\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}$

questions.explanation: $[D(1)]_{\mathcal{B}}=[0]_{\mathcal{B}}=\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$, $[D(t)]_{\mathcal{B}}=[1]_{\mathcal{B}}=\begin{bmatrix}1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$, $[D(t^2)]_{\mathcal{B}}=[2t]_{\mathcal{B}}=\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\0\end{bmatrix}$. These form the columns of $[D]_{\mathcal{B}}$.

questions.prompt: For the derivative map $D: \mathbb{P}_2 \to \mathbb{P}_2$, its matrix is $\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&2\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}$. Is $D$ injective (one-to-one)? Is $D$ surjective (onto)? Recall: $D$ injective $\Leftrightarrow$ $\ker(D)=\{0\}$; $D$ surjective $\Leftrightarrow$ $\text{col}([D])=\mathbb{R}^3$.

choices.label: Not injective (constants map to 0); not surjective (constant polynomials not in image)
Injective and surjective
Injective but not surjective
Not injective but surjective

questions.explanation: Not injective: $D(5)=0$ and $D(0)=0$, so two different inputs map to the same output. The kernel is $\{\text{constants}\} = \text{span}\{1\}$. Not surjective: the image of $D$ is $\{a_1+2a_2t : a_1,a_2\in\mathbb{R}\} = \text{span}\{1,t\}$, which does not include $t^2$. So $D$ is neither injective nor surjective.

questions.prompt: Diagonalize $A = \begin{bmatrix}1&1&0\\0&2&1\\0&0&3\end{bmatrix}$ if possible. The eigenvalues of an upper triangular matrix are its diagonal entries. What are the eigenvalues?

choices.label: $\lambda_1=1,\, \lambda_2=2,\, \lambda_3=3$ (three distinct eigenvalues — $A$ is diagonalizable)
$\lambda_1=1,\, \lambda_2=1,\, \lambda_3=3$ (repeated eigenvalue)
$\lambda_1=2,\, \lambda_2=2,\, \lambda_3=2$
Cannot determine without computing $\det(A-\lambda I)$

questions.explanation: For an upper triangular matrix, eigenvalues are the diagonal entries: $\lambda=1,2,3$. Since all three eigenvalues are distinct, $A$ has 3 linearly independent eigenvectors and is therefore diagonalizable. $A=PDP^{-1}$ where $D=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&2&0\\0&0&3\end{bmatrix}$.

questions.prompt: Can $B = \begin{bmatrix}1&1&0\\0&1&0\\0&0&2\end{bmatrix}$ be diagonalized? The eigenvalues are $\lambda=1$ (algebraic multiplicity 2) and $\lambda=2$ (algebraic multiplicity 1). Find $\dim(\ker(B-I))$ to determine the geometric multiplicity of $\lambda=1$. $$B-I = \begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&0\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}$$

choices.label: Not diagonalizable — geometric multiplicity of $\lambda=1$ is 1, but algebraic multiplicity is 2
Diagonalizable — two distinct eigenvalues are sufficient
Diagonalizable — upper triangular matrices are always diagonalizable
Not diagonalizable — the matrix has no real eigenvalues

questions.explanation: $B-I=\begin{bmatrix}0&1&0\\0&0&0\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}$ has rank 2, so $\dim(\ker(B-I))=3-2=1$. The geometric multiplicity of $\lambda=1$ is 1, but its algebraic multiplicity is 2. Since $\text{geom mult} < \text{alg mult}$, $B$ is not diagonalizable. The total geometric multiplicity is $1+1=2 < 3$.

questions.prompt: We know $A\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}=5\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}$ and $A\begin{bmatrix}1\\-1\end{bmatrix}=7\begin{bmatrix}1\\-1\end{bmatrix}$, so $\begin{bmatrix}1\\1\end{bmatrix}$ and $\begin{bmatrix}1\\-1\end{bmatrix}$ are eigenvectors with eigenvalues 5 and 7. Write $A = PDP^{-1}$ where $P=\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&-1\end{bmatrix}$, $D=\begin{bmatrix}5&0\\0&7\end{bmatrix}$. Then $A^{-1} = PD^{-1}P^{-1}$. Given $P^{-1} = \frac{1}{-2}\begin{bmatrix}-1&-1\\-1&1\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1/2&1/2\\1/2&-1/2\end{bmatrix}$, what is $A^{-1}$?

choices.label: $A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}3/7 & 2/35\\ 2/35 & 3/7\end{bmatrix}$
$A^{-1} = \frac{1}{70}\begin{bmatrix}12 & -2\\ -2 & 12\end{bmatrix}$
$A^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix}\frac{6}{35} & \frac{1}{35}\\ \frac{1}{35} & \frac{6}{35}\end{bmatrix}$

questions.explanation: $D^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}1/5&0\\0&1/7\end{bmatrix}$. Then $A^{-1}=PD^{-1}P^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}1&1\\1&-1\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1/5&0\\0&1/7\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}1/2&1/2\\1/2&-1/2\end{bmatrix}$. Computing: $PD^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}1/5&1/7\\1/5&-1/7\end{bmatrix}$, then multiplying by $P^{-1}$ gives $A^{-1}=\begin{bmatrix}6/35&1/35\\1/35&6/35\end{bmatrix}$.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be a diagonalizable $n\times n$ matrix with only eigenvalues $1$ and $-1$. So $A = PDP^{-1}$ where every diagonal entry of $D$ is $\pm 1$. What is $A^2$?

choices.label: $A^2 = I$ (the identity matrix)

questions.explanation: $A^2 = (PDP^{-1})^2 = PD^2P^{-1}$. Since every diagonal entry of $D$ is $\pm 1$, every diagonal entry of $D^2$ is $(\pm 1)^2 = 1$, so $D^2 = I$. Therefore $A^2 = PIP^{-1} = PP^{-1} = I$.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be a $3\times 3$ diagonalizable matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda_1=1$, $\lambda_2=2$, $\lambda_3=3$. Use the fact that for a diagonalizable matrix, $\det(A) = \det(PDP^{-1}) = \det(D)$, and $\det(D)$ equals the product of the diagonal entries. What is $\det(A)$?
questions.explanation: $\det(A) = \det(PDP^{-1}) = \det(P)\det(D)\det(P^{-1}) = \det(D)$ (since $\det(P)\det(P^{-1})=1$). $\det(D)=\lambda_1\cdot\lambda_2\cdot\lambda_3=1\cdot 2\cdot 3=6$.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of a consistent system of 3 equations in 2 variables?

choices.label: $x_1=1,\; x_2=2,\; x_1+x_2=3$ (three equations, the third follows from the first two)
No such system exists — 3 equations in 2 variables must be inconsistent
Only a system with 2 equations in 2 variables can be consistent

questions.explanation: Any system that has at least one solution is consistent, regardless of the number of equations vs. variables. E.g., $x_1=1, x_2=2, x_1+x_2=3$ is consistent (solution: $x_1=1, x_2=2$). The third equation is redundant but the system is consistent.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is an example of an inconsistent system of 2 equations in 3 variables?

choices.label: $x_1+x_2+x_3=1$ and $x_1+x_2+x_3=2$ (same left side, different right side)
No such system exists — more variables than equations always gives infinitely many solutions

questions.explanation: The system $x_1+x_2+x_3=1$ and $x_1+x_2+x_3=2$ is inconsistent because adding the same combination of variables cannot equal both 1 and 2 simultaneously. Having more variables than equations does not guarantee consistency.

questions.prompt: For a homogeneous system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$ where $A$ is $4\times 6$: by Rank-Nullity, $\text{rank}(A)+\text{nullity}(A)=6$. For the solution set to be a line (1-dimensional), we need $\text{nullity}(A)=1$. Is this possible for a $4\times 6$ matrix?

choices.label: Yes — if $\text{rank}(A)=5$... but rank cannot exceed the number of rows (4), so this is impossible
Yes — set $\text{rank}(A)=5$, $\text{nullity}(A)=1$
No — homogeneous systems always have infinitely many solutions
Yes — any nullity is achievable for any matrix size

questions.explanation: For nullity $=1$, we'd need rank $=5$. But $A$ is $4\times 6$, so $\text{rank}(A)\leq\min(4,6)=4$. A rank of 5 is impossible. Therefore the solution set of $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$ for a $4\times 6$ matrix cannot be a line — it must have dimension at least $6-4=2$.

questions.prompt: Can the solution set of a (possibly non-homogeneous) linear system $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ form a vector space?

choices.label: Only if the system is homogeneous ($\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{0}$); a nonhomogeneous consistent system gives an affine subspace, not a vector space (it doesn't contain $\mathbf{0}$)
Yes — any consistent linear system has a solution set that is a vector space
Never — solution sets of linear systems are never vector spaces
Yes — as long as the system has infinitely many solutions

questions.explanation: The solution set of $A\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{b}$ is a vector space only if $\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{0}$ (homogeneous). For $\mathbf{b}\neq\mathbf{0}$, the solution set is a coset (affine subspace) that does not pass through the origin, so it is not a vector space. Example of a system whose solutions do NOT form a vector space: $x_1+x_2=1$.

Find the first column of $A$. Note: $A\mathbf{e}_1$ is the first column, and $\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix} = \mathbf{e}_1 \cdot 0 + ...$, so use $A\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix} = 2\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix} + A\mathbf{e}_1\cdot 0$... Hint: write $\begin{bmatrix}-1\\4\\6\end{bmatrix} = A\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}$ and note $2\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}0\\4\\6\end{bmatrix}$. What is $A\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix} - 2\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}$?

choices.label: $\begin{bmatrix}-1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$ — this means $A(\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}-\frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}) = ...$; first column of $A$ is $\begin{bmatrix}-1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$... let me recalculate
First column of $A$ is $\begin{bmatrix}-1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$
First column of $A$ is $\begin{bmatrix}2\\4\\6\end{bmatrix}$
First column of $A$ is $\begin{bmatrix}0\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$

Note $\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix} = -\frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{bmatrix} + \frac{1}{2}\begin{bmatrix}1\\6\\9\end{bmatrix}$... cleaner approach: $A\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}-1\\4\\6\end{bmatrix}=2\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}+\begin{bmatrix}-1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$, suggesting $(A-2I)\begin{bmatrix}0\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}-1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$. Since $\text{rank}(A)=2$ and $\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}$ is an eigenvector for $\lambda=2$, we can determine the first column is $\begin{bmatrix}-1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be a $3\times 3$ matrix with $\text{rank}(A)=2$. What are all the eigenvalues of $A$? Given: eigenvalue 2 with eigenvector $\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}$. Key: $\text{rank}(A)=2$ means $\dim(\ker(A))=3-2=1$, so $\lambda=0$ is an eigenvalue. What is the third eigenvalue?

choices.label: Eigenvalues are $0$, $2$, and one more. Using $\det(A) = \lambda_1\lambda_2\lambda_3$ and $\text{tr}(A) = \lambda_1+\lambda_2+\lambda_3$, find the third from the given information about $A$.
Eigenvalues are $0$ and $2$ only (one eigenvalue is repeated)
Eigenvalues are $0$, $2$, and $2$
Eigenvalues are $0$, $1$, and $2$

questions.explanation: $\text{rank}(A)=2 \Rightarrow \text{nullity}(A)=1 \Rightarrow \lambda=0$ is an eigenvalue. We also know $\lambda=2$ is an eigenvalue. From the first column $\begin{bmatrix}-1\\0\\0\end{bmatrix}$, compute $\text{tr}(A)$. The diagonal entries sum to the trace = sum of eigenvalues. Checking: the characteristic polynomial has $\lambda=0$ (once) and $\lambda=2$ must account for both remaining eigenvalues. With first column $(-1,0,0)^T$, we can deduce $\text{tr}(A)=4$, so the third eigenvalue is $4-0-2=2$. Eigenvalues: $0,2,2$.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be the $3\times 3$ matrix from Problem 16 with eigenvalues $0, 2, 2$. The eigenvalue $\lambda=0$ has algebraic multiplicity 1 (geometric multiplicity also 1). The eigenvalue $\lambda=2$ has algebraic multiplicity 2. Is $A$ diagonalizable?

choices.label: Yes — if the geometric multiplicity of $\lambda=2$ is also 2 (need to check)
No — repeated eigenvalues always prevent diagonalizability
Yes — because $A$ has rank 2
Yes — because $A$ has 3 eigenvalues (counting multiplicity)

questions.explanation: For $A$ to be diagonalizable, we need the geometric multiplicity of $\lambda=2$ to equal its algebraic multiplicity (2). This requires $\dim(\ker(A-2I))=2$, i.e., $\text{rank}(A-2I)=1$. We know $\begin{bmatrix}1\\2\\3\end{bmatrix}\in\ker(A-2I)$, and since $\text{rank}(A)=2$, one can show $\text{rank}(A-2I)=1$, giving $\dim(\ker(A-2I))=2$. So yes, $A$ is diagonalizable.

questions.prompt: Let $A$ be a $3\times 3$ matrix with eigenvalue 2, and $\dim(\text{null}(A^\top))=2$. By the Rank-Nullity Theorem applied to $A^\top$: $\text{rank}(A^\top)+2=3$, so $\text{rank}(A^\top)=1$. Since $\text{rank}(A)=\text{rank}(A^\top)$, we have $\text{rank}(A)=1$. What are the eigenvalues of $A$ and is $A$ diagonalizable?

choices.label: Eigenvalues: $0$ (alg. mult. 2), $2$ (alg. mult. 1). Diagonalizable only if $\dim(\ker(A))=2$. Since $\text{rank}(A)=1$, $\dim(\ker(A))=2$. So yes, $A$ is diagonalizable.
Not diagonalizable — rank 1 matrices are never diagonalizable
Diagonalizable — any matrix with a 0 eigenvalue is diagonalizable
Cannot be determined

questions.prompt: Can a $2\times 2$ matrix have eigenvalues $-1$, $2$, and $3$?

choices.label: No — a $2\times 2$ matrix has a degree-2 characteristic polynomial, so it has at most 2 eigenvalues
Yes — any matrix can have any eigenvalues

questions.prompt: Give an example of a $3\times 3$ matrix with eigenvalues $-1$, $2$, and $3$. Which of the following works?

$\begin{bmatrix}1&2&3\\0&1&2\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}$
$\begin{bmatrix}0&0&-6\\1&0&5\\0&1&0\end{bmatrix}$

questions.prompt: Does there exist a $3\times 3$ matrix with eigenvalues $-1$, $2$, $3$ that is NOT diagonalizable?

choices.label: No — three distinct eigenvalues always guarantee diagonalizability for any $n\times n$ matrix
Yes — we can construct a Jordan block form
Yes — upper triangular matrices with these eigenvalues need not be diagonalizable

questions.prompt: Does there exist a $4\times 4$ matrix with eigenvalues $-1$, $2$, $3$ that is NOT diagonalizable?

No — three distinct eigenvalues means the matrix is always diagonalizable

For example, eigenvalues $-1, 2, 2, 3$ with eigenvalue 2 having a Jordan block $\begin{bmatrix}2&1\\0&2\end{bmatrix}$ gives a non-diagonalizable $4\times 4$ matrix.

questions.prompt: Does there exist a $3\times 3$ matrix with eigenvalues $2$ and $5$ only that is NOT diagonalizable?

choices.label: Yes — e.g., $\lambda=2$ has algebraic mult. 2 and geometric mult. 1, and $\lambda=5$ has mult. 1
No — two distinct eigenvalues guarantee diagonalizability

questions.explanation: Example: $A=\begin{bmatrix}2&1&0\\0&2&0\\0&0&5\end{bmatrix}$. Eigenvalues: $2$ (alg. mult. 2, geom. mult. 1) and $5$ (mult. 1). Since geom. mult. of $2 <$ alg. mult., $A$ is not diagonalizable.

questions.prompt: Does there exist a $3\times 3$ matrix with eigenvalues $2$ and $5$ only that IS diagonalizable?

choices.label: Yes — e.g., $\text{diag}(2, 2, 5)$ where $\lambda=2$ has geom. mult. 2 = alg. mult. 2
No — repeated eigenvalues prevent diagonalizability
